concept_name: Distance vs Displacement — Path Length vs Straight-Line Change
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 6.1 — Motion in a Straight Line; split from distance_vs_displacement bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: Taking an autorickshaw across a busy Indian city. The meter charges by distance — the actual winding path, every turn around a stalled truck, every detour to avoid a closed road. Your GPS app shows something different — the straight-line DISPLACEMENT from your start address to your destination. The meter number is almost always larger than the GPS-straight-line, and only equal if the route is perfectly straight with no reversals.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A child running one full lap around the circular track at a school sports day. The running track is four hundred metres around — that is the distance. But the child ends where she started, so her displacement is zero. Same motion, two completely different answers depending on which quantity you measure.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A postman delivering letters on your lane. He walks to house number fifty, then doubles back to forty, then forward to seventy. His total distance walked is long; his displacement by end-of-round is just the gap between his starting point and the last house visited.

ratio.formula: (2 * PI * radius * arc_fraction) / (2 * radius * sin(PI * arc_fraction) + 1e-9)

formulas.distance: distance = total path length covered (scalar, always >= 0)
formulas.displacement: displacement = r_final - r_initial (vector; sign matters in 1D, direction matters in 2D)

constraints: Distance can never decrease during motion; it only accumulates
Displacement can decrease, increase, OR return to zero
Equality holds ONLY when the motion is a straight line with no reversals

STATE_1.title: An Autorickshaw Ride — Two Different Numbers for the Same Trip

scene_composition.text:
The auto meter reads
~ 5 km
(total path length = DISTANCE)

Your GPS app shows
~ 3 km
(straight-line = DISPLACEMENT)

Same trip. Two different numbers.
The physics distinction matters.

tts_sentences.text_en: You take an autorickshaw from home to office. The meter ticks up the whole ride — past a stalled truck, around a blocked road, two left turns and a U-turn.
At the end, the meter shows five kilometres. But your maps app had said the destination is only three kilometres away.
Both numbers are correct. They measure different things. The meter's five kilometres is the DISTANCE — the total winding path the auto actually traced.
The three kilometres from your app is the DISPLACEMENT — the straight-line gap between start and end, ignoring the route.
In physics these are two distinct quantities. One is a scalar, the other is a vector. The next three states make the difference precise.

STATE_2.title: Definitions — Scalar vs Vector, Path vs Endpoint

scene_composition.text:
DISTANCE (scalar)

Total path length covered.
Add up every tiny step,
regardless of direction.

  d  >=  0  always
  d  never decreases
  units: metres, kilometres

DISPLACEMENT (vector)

Straight arrow from start to end.
Only endpoints matter.
Middle of the path is irrelevant.

  s = r_final - r_initial
  can be +, -, or zero
  units: metres, kilometres
        PLUS a direction
Definitions — Scalar vs Vector, Path vs Endpoint

tts_sentences.text_en: Here are the precise definitions, side by side.

Distance is the total length of path traced — a scalar. Add up every tiny step, regardless of the direction you were moving. Distance is always greater than or equal to zero, and it never decreases once motion starts.
Displacement is a vector — the straight-line arrow from your starting position to your final position. Only the two endpoints matter. The middle of the journey plays no role.
Displacement carries a direction, so it can be positive, negative, or zero depending on the sign convention and the ending point.

STATE_3.title: The Extreme Case — Complete Lap Has Displacement ZERO

scene_composition.text:
__________
           __/          \__
         /                  \
        |       TRACK        |
        |      (400 m         |
         \     circumference)/
           \_____________/

              START = END
              (same spot)

One full lap around the track

Distance covered
  = full circumference
  = 400 m

Displacement
  = straight arrow from
    START to END
  = START and END are
    the SAME point
  = ZERO

Distance: 400 m
Displacement: 0

Same motion. Totally different answers.
The Extreme Case — Complete Lap Has Displacement ZERO

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the most extreme example. A child runs one full lap around a four-hundred-metre track at her school sports day.
She ends exactly where she started — at the starting line.
Her distance covered is the full circumference, four hundred metres. Her feet traced every metre of the track.
But her displacement is zero. Start and end are the same spot, so the straight-line arrow has length zero.
Same motion. Two completely different answers. This is the single most important reason physics distinguishes distance from displacement.
The general rule — for any curved or reversing path, distance is always more than the magnitude of displacement. Equality holds only when motion is straight-line without reversals.

STATE_4.title: The Inequality You Will Use Forever

scene_composition.text:
Universal rule

   |displacement|  <=  distance

Equality (|s| = d) holds ONLY when
  (1) motion is in a straight line AND
  (2) no reversal of direction

Strict inequality (|s| < d) whenever
  - path is curved, OR
  - direction reverses

Quick examples

A -> B in straight line, 10 m
   dist = 10, |disp| = 10   (equal)

A -> B -> A, each 10 m
   dist = 20, |disp| = 0

Semicircle of radius 5 m
   dist = pi*5 ~ 15.7 m
   |disp| = diameter = 10 m

Full circle of radius r
   dist = 2*pi*r,  |disp| = 0
The Inequality You Will Use Forever

tts_sentences.text_en: The universal rule — the magnitude of displacement is always less than or equal to the distance. Never greater.
Equality — distance equals displacement magnitude — holds only when two conditions are met. The path is straight, and the direction never reverses.
Any curve, any U-turn, any back-and-forth motion — and the distance exceeds the displacement magnitude.
Some quick examples to lock this in. A straight ten-metre walk — distance and displacement are both ten. A walk ten metres forward, then ten back — distance is twenty, displacement is zero.
A half-circle of radius five — distance is pi times five, about fifteen point seven metres, while displacement is just the diameter, ten metres.
Apply this rule every time a problem involves paths that are not straight. The two quantities do not interchange — they are genuinely different measurements.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Distance and displacement are always the same quantity
trigger_phrases: aren't distance and displacement the same
why different names for same thing

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

I walked 5 km.
I am 5 km from where I started.

Aren't those the same number?
Why do we need two different
words for one thing?

(The assumption only works when
the walk was perfectly straight.
Let us break the assumption and see.)
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A very common first-instinct. I walked five kilometres, so I am five kilometres from where I started. Distance and displacement feel like the same thing.
That feels true because you are imagining a perfectly straight walk. The moment the path curves or reverses, the two quantities separate.
Let us break the straight-line assumption and watch what happens.

STATE_2.title: Go Out and Come Back — Distance Keeps Growing, Displacement Returns

scene_composition.text:
After the walk

Distance covered
  10 km out + 10 km back
  = 20 km  (keeps adding)

Displacement
  start and end are the same
  = 0

20 km vs 0 km.
Same motion. Two very
different answers.
Hence two different names.

tts_sentences.text_en: Walk ten kilometres down a road, then turn and walk the same ten kilometres back home.
Your feet covered twenty kilometres — that is the distance. You were tired after walking twenty.
But the straight-line gap between your start and end is zero — you are back where you began. So the displacement is zero.
Twenty and zero are plainly different numbers. That is why physics needs two different names — because the two measurements give genuinely different values for any non-straight motion.

STATE_3.title: When the Two Do Match — The Strict Straight-Line Case

scene_composition.text:
ONLY case where
distance = |displacement|

Motion is straight-line AND
direction never reverses.

Examples
- rocket going up, no return
- ball falling, before it bounces
- train on straight track,
  forward only

In every other situation,
distance > |displacement|.
When the Two Do Match — The Strict Straight-Line Case

tts_sentences.text_en: The two quantities do agree in one specific situation — straight-line motion with no reversal.
A rocket climbing straight up before it falls back. A ball falling before it bounces. A train on a straight track moving only forward.
In all these cases, distance and displacement magnitude give the same number. But note the conditions — straight AND no reversal. Miss either and the equality breaks.
In every other situation, distance is strictly greater than the magnitude of displacement. That gap is where the two names earn their keep.

regeneration_variants.label: Semicircle path
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Particle moves from A to B along a semicircle of radius 5 m. distance = pi*5 = 15.71 m, |displacement| = 2*5 = 10 m, ratio = pi/2.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same inequality |disp| <= dist, strict when path is curved

regeneration_variants.label: Out-and-back with unequal legs
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Go 10 m east, then 6 m west. Distance = 16 m. Displacement = 4 m east. Magnitude 4 m < distance 16 m.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Any reversal makes distance exceed displacement magnitude
